common_name: Delfino Cilantro (Feathery Fernlike-Leaf Cilantro Variety for Ornamental and Culinary Use)

planting.note: Direct sow only — cilantro does not transplant successfully; sow in early spring several weeks before last frost or in fall in zone 9. In zone 9, the best planting window is October–November for fall-winter leaf production (the optimal season), with a short spring crop sown February–March before bolting from summer heat. Delfino Cilantro is a named variety selected for its distinctively different leaf form: rather than the typical broad, flat lobed cilantro leaves, Delfino produces very finely divided, feathery, fernlike foliage similar in appearance to dill. The flavor is classic cilantro with the same citrus-soapy aromatic profile of standard varieties; the texture and appearance are quite different and make Delfino particularly attractive for garnishing, edible landscaping, and situations where visual distinction from standard flat-leaf cilantro is desired. Delfino also shows reasonably good slow-to-bolt characteristics.

directions: Direct sow thinly in cool soil; thin to 6 inches. Harvest young leaves before bolting for best flavor; allow some plants to flower for seed production. Delfino Cilantro is a named selection of Coriandrum sativum distinguished by its unusual leaf morphology: while standard cilantro produces broad, somewhat lobed leaves resembling flat-leaf parsley at the base, Delfino produces very finely divided, thread-like foliage that resembles dill or fennel fronds far more than it resembles conventional cilantro. This leaf form is caused by a genetic trait affecting leaf development — Delfino consistently produces only the finely dissected leaf form throughout its growth, never the broad lower leaves typical of standard cilantro. The flavor and aroma are identical to standard cilantro: the same distinctive volatile compounds (primarily linalool and various aldehydes) that give cilantro its characteristic citrusy, herbaceous, and for some people soap-like quality are present in the same proportion in Delfino as in standard types. The feathery foliage has several culinary advantages: the very fine texture integrates easily into salads and garnishes, the delicate fronds are visually striking on finished dishes, and the fine cut of the leaves releases aromatic compounds easily when torn or chopped. For zone 9 gardeners growing cilantro in fall and early spring gardens, Delfino provides a visually distinctive alternative to standard varieties with the same flavor and culture requirements. Like all cilantro, Delfino is a host plant for black swallowtail butterfly caterpillars, which feed on cilantro, dill, fennel, and related Apiaceae plants.
sources: Renee's Garden Seeds — Delfino cilantro feathery-leaf variety notes
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Specialty cilantro variety comparison including Delfino leaf type
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cilantro and Coriander Production in Florida

common_name: Leisure Cilantro (Classic Open-Pollinated Slow-Bolt Cilantro for Leaf Production)

planting.note: Direct sow only in cool soil; does not transplant well. In zone 9, plant October–November for fall-winter production or February–March for a brief spring crop. Leisure is one of the classic open-pollinated slow-bolt cilantro varieties — selected specifically for delayed bolting (the rush to flower and seed that ends leaf production) compared to standard varieties. The 'slow-bolt' trait means Leisure stays in leaf-producing mode for a longer period under warming conditions than standard cilantro varieties, extending the productive harvest window in spring gardens. This is particularly valuable in zone 9 where the spring temperature rise is rapid and cilantro planted in late February may have only a short window before bolting; Leisure's extended slow-bolt characteristic can add 2–3 weeks of productive leaf harvest in spring conditions. The leaves are typical broad, flat cilantro foliage with the standard cilantro flavor.

directions: Direct sow thinly in cool soil; thin to 6–9 inches. Harvest outer leaves successively before bolting. Leisure Cilantro is one of the most widely recommended open-pollinated cilantro varieties for home gardeners specifically wanting maximum leaf production before the plant bolts to seed. All cilantro (Coriandrum sativum) is genetically programmed to flower — it is an annual that must complete its seed cycle — but the timing and speed of this transition from vegetative (leaf-producing) growth to reproductive (flower/seed) growth varies considerably among cultivated varieties, and selecting for delayed bolting has been a primary breeding objective in cilantro improvement since leaf herb production became the primary commercial use of the crop in North America. Leisure was selected from open-pollinated cilantro populations for its consistent tendency to remain in vegetative leaf production mode longer than standard unselected populations, particularly as temperatures increase in spring. This character is not the same as the more dramatically extended bolt resistance of F1 hybrid varieties like Calypso, but it is meaningfully better than generic cilantro seed and substantially better than truly fast-bolting varieties. For zone 9 gardeners, the distinction matters: in the rapid spring temperature rise of the Gulf Coast (February through April), even 2–3 extra weeks of leaf production before bolting is a meaningful difference in total harvest. Leisure's open-pollinated status means seed can be saved from selected late-bolting individual plants to potentially improve the variety further for local conditions over successive generations.
sources: Territorial Seed Company — Leisure cilantro slow-bolt variety notes
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Open-pollinated slow-bolt cilantro variety comparison
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Cilantro Production Recommendations for Gulf Coast Gardens

common_name: Calypso Cilantro (F1 Hybrid Bolt-Resistant Cilantro with Extended Leaf Production Season)

planting.note: Direct sow only; cilantro does not transplant well. Calypso is the most bolt-resistant of the modern F1 hybrid cilantro varieties, extending leaf production significantly beyond what open-pollinated slow-bolt varieties can achieve. In zone 9, plant October–November for fall-winter production or February–March for spring. Calypso's extended bolt resistance means it can continue producing harvestable leaves even as temperatures climb into the 70s and low 80s°F — conditions that trigger bolting in standard varieties within days to weeks. For zone 9 spring production, Calypso provides the longest productive harvest window of any commonly available cilantro variety. The trade-off of F1 hybrid status is that saved seed will not reproduce Calypso's characteristics reliably — gardeners must purchase fresh seed each season. Calypso was developed by Johnny's Selected Seeds.

directions: Direct sow in cool soil; thin to 6 inches. Harvest outer leaves continuously; Calypso's extended bolt resistance allows repeated harvests well into warming spring weather. Calypso is an F1 hybrid cilantro variety developed and released by Johnny's Selected Seeds, the Maine-based seed company known for developing and trialing specialty vegetable varieties for commercial and home garden use. The variety represents the current apex of bolt-resistance breeding in cilantro — the characteristic that most limits cilantro's usefulness in spring and fall gardens, where temperatures inevitably warm and trigger the plant's transition from vegetative leaf production to reproductive flowering and seed set. In university and industry trials comparing cilantro varieties for bolt resistance, Calypso consistently produces the most leaf material and maintains quality leaf production the longest under warming conditions. The mechanism of Calypso's extended bolt resistance involves both the genetic selection for delayed photoperiod and temperature sensitivity in triggering the flowering response, and the F1 hybrid vigor that produces more robust vegetative growth generally. Calypso plants are somewhat taller and more vigorous than many open-pollinated varieties, producing more leaf material per plant in addition to bolting more slowly. The flavor is standard cilantro — the same characteristic aromatic profile from the volatile oils common to all Coriandrum sativum — with no meaningful flavor difference from other cilantro varieties. For zone 9 spring gardens where the cilantro harvest window is compressed by rapid warming, Calypso is the most productive choice among commonly available varieties; in the fall-winter planting season (October–March in zone 9), when temperatures are consistently cool, the bolt-resistance advantage is less important and open-pollinated varieties perform nearly as well.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Calypso F1 cilantro development and bolt-resistance trial data
University of California Cooperative Extension — Cilantro Variety Comparison for Extended Season Production
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Cool-Season Herb Production for Gulf Coast Home Gardens

common_name: Top Crop Bush Bean (AAS-Winning Classic Heirloom Bush Snap Bean, Early and Dependable)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is at least 60°F; beans rot in cold, wet soil. Top Crop is a classic early bush snap bean that can also tolerate warm conditions — it was originally selected and trialed specifically for heat resistance and productivity under the warm, humid conditions of the southeastern United States and received an All-America Selections award in 1951 for its superior performance over existing bush bean varieties. In zone 9, direct sow March–May for spring production and August–September for fall production; bean plants decline quickly when summer temperatures consistently exceed 95°F but spring and fall crops can be very productive. The plants are compact and upright (18–24 inches), producing straight, round, stringless or nearly stringless pods 5–6 inches long in abundance at 49–54 days.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost; space 4–6 inches in rows 18 inches apart. Harvest at 5–6 inches before seeds bulge. Top Crop is one of the historically significant All-America Selections winners in the vegetable category, recognized in 1951 for performance and quality that exceeded the bush bean standard of the era. At the time of its AAS recognition, Top Crop represented a meaningful improvement over other widely grown bush bean varieties in terms of pod quality (straight, stringless, and uniform), plant habit (upright and compact, making harvest easier), and importantly for southeastern gardens — its tolerance for warm, humid growing conditions that cause many bean varieties to set pods poorly or produce stringy, misshapen pods. The variety became one of the most widely grown bush snap bean varieties in American home and market gardens through the 1950s, 1960s, and 1970s, establishing itself as a regional standard especially in the South where the heat tolerance advantage mattered most. Top Crop produces round, stringless pods (the 'Top Crop' designation reflects the goal of beans that crop densely at the top of the plant, making harvest efficient) that are firm, tender at optimal harvest size (5–6 inches), and of classic, straightforward snap bean flavor. The variety is open-pollinated and remains available through seed companies as a historical heirloom standard. For zone 9 spring and fall gardens, Top Crop's heat tolerance and early maturity (49–54 days) make it practical for getting a bean crop harvested before summer heat peaks or after it subsides in fall.
sources: All-America Selections — Top Crop bush bean 1951 AAS recognition documentation
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Top Crop heirloom bush bean variety notes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Bush Bean Variety Recommendations for Gulf South Home Gardens

common_name: Walking Stick Collard (Tall Perennial Heirloom Collard from the Carolina and Gulf South Tradition)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; in zone 9, start seeds in August for September transplanting ahead of fall. Walking Stick Collard is a truly perennial (or at minimum biennial) collard that develops an upright, thick stem over multiple years of growth — eventually reaching 4–8 feet in height with a distinctive woody stem that in the South was historically harvested for use as a walking stick, giving the variety its name. Unlike annual collard varieties grown as single-season crops, Walking Stick Collard can remain productive for 2–5 years in zone 9's mild winters, producing new growth from the upper portion of the plant as it elongates. Individual leaves are harvested from the lower portion of the stem as new growth continues from the top. In zone 9, the plants perform well through winter (tolerating multiple frosts and brief freezes) and may slow in summer heat but rarely die entirely, giving them a distinctly perennial character that sets them apart from annual collard varieties. Space widely (24–36 inches) to allow each plant to develop its multi-year structure.

directions: Start seeds indoors; transplant to permanent location and allow to develop over multiple seasons. Harvest outer lower leaves as needed year-round. Walking Stick Collard is one of the most historically distinctive vegetables of the American South — a tall, perennial collard type that was maintained for generations in family gardens across the Carolinas, Georgia, and the Gulf South as a semi-permanent garden plant rather than an annual crop. The 'walking stick' in the name refers to the thick, woody stems that develop as the plant grows upward over multiple years: historical accounts describe Southern farmers and homesteaders harvesting old Walking Stick Collard stems, drying them, and using them as walking sticks, canes, and tool handles — a practical byproduct of a plant grown primarily for its edible leaves. The leaves are classic collard — large, blue-green, thick, and of the rich, mild-bitter flavor that defines Southern greens cuisine — harvested continuously from the lower stem as the plant elongates upward. A well-established Walking Stick Collard plant can produce leaves continuously for 3–5 years in zone 9's mild climate, making it a true perennial food plant for gardens where winter rarely kills brassicas completely. The variety is maintained by seed savers and small heirloom seed companies as a Southern heritage vegetable; it is not available in commercial mass-market seed catalogs but has been preserved through the living tradition of Southern kitchen gardens and organizations like Seed Savers Exchange and Southern Exposure Seed Exchange. Walking Stick Collard's extraordinary height (sometimes reaching 6–8 feet) and its multi-year productive life make it one of the most distinctive and visually arresting vegetables possible in zone 9 gardens, forming a structural element with year-round productivity that no annual collard variety can match.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Walking Stick Collard Southern heirloom perennial collard documentation
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Walking Stick Collard heritage vegetable variety notes
North Carolina State University Extension — Heirloom Collard Varieties of the Carolinas and Gulf South
